ch1-scene0: A narrow, dimly-lit attic corridor opens to reveal a young man descending a cramped, peeling staircase. The oppressive heat seeps through dusty windows, casting harsh amber light over battered, paint-flecked walls. The young man, tall, gaunt, with unruly dark hair and threadbare, out-of-fashion clothing, moves warily to avoid the open kitchen door below, behind which a stern landlady can be glimpsed among shadows. The atmosphere is tense and claustrophobic, embodying St. Petersburg in the mid-19th century.
ch1-scene1: A bustling, grimy St. Petersburg street in the sweltering summer twilight, lined with crooked buildings and scaffolding. Workers pass by in linen shirts and caps, while disheveled townsfolk mingle with drunken figures. Raskolnikov, in his shabby coat and battered hat, stands amidst the dust and confusion, his tall, thin frame lost and anxious in the chaos. A heavy waggon pulled by a dray horse passes by, carrying a raucous drunk, while the stench of pot-houses and the close air flattens the scene.
ch1-scene2: At the grimy base of a massive tenement building typical of 19th-century St. Petersburg, Raskolnikov slips unnoticed through a shadowy side door. The back staircase is dark, narrow, and heavily worn, lit by faint daylight filtering in from a landing. Porters in work clothes maneuver heavy furniture down the passage, while Raskolnikov, tense and cautious, sneaks past them. The atmosphere is close and anonymous, the corridors echoing with the sounds of labor and movement from the occupants.
ch1-scene3: A cramped, meticulously tidy 19th-century Petersburg flat, washed in the golden, slanted light of the setting sun. Flowered muslin curtains, scrubbed yellow floors, and a few austere pieces of yellow-wood furniture fill the room, with a polished table and an ikon lamp burning softly in the corner. Alyona Ivanovna, small, frail, with sharp features and oily grizzled hair, stands in the center, wrapped in a faded fur cape, scrutinizing a hesitating Raskolnikov. He stands awkwardly near the door, clutching a battered watch, both figures dressed in modest, period-appropriate but worn attire. The air is thick with suspicion and poverty.
ch1-scene4: Raskolnikov stumbles dazedly down the dim, narrow stairwell of a crowded Petersburg tenement, the shadows broken by slivers of fading daylight from a stairwell window. Emerging onto the busy street, he lurches among indifferent passersby, his expression twisted with agitation and self-loathing. The evening is humid and oppressive, with the city's squalor pressed into every cobblestone and facade.
ch1-scene5: A gloomy, low-ceilinged basement tavern cluttered with sticky wooden tables, yellowed from years of smoke. Dim, warm candlelight reflects off dark walls and cheap glassware. Several patrons linger: Raskolnikov sits hunched in the far corner, drinking beer, a reddish flush on his pale face; two drunken men clumsily support each other near the steps; an artisan, slumped in a faded coat, sips beer next to his burly, bearded companion who sleeps fitfully on the bench; a solitary, anxious-looking man in faded civil servant garb occupies another table. The atmosphere is thick with the haze of drink and hardship characteristic of St. Petersburg’s underbelly.
ch2-scene0: A 19th-century Russian tavern interior, dimly lit by a few candles, with rough wooden tables and benches. Raskolnikov, thin and disheveled, sits at a table, his expression weary. Semyon Zaharovitch Marmeladov, an older, bloated man in a ragged black coat with a stained waistcoat, sits nearby, soon moving to sit beside Raskolnikov. At the counter, a fourteen-year-old boy in shabby clothes stands, while a younger boy attends to serving food and drink. The master/innkeeper, a burly man with oil-smeared features and tarred boots, occasionally enters from a side room. Other rough-looking patrons linger; the grimy atmosphere is thick with smoke and drink.
ch2-scene1: Inside the cramped, candlelit tavern, with wooden tables crowded by a mix of tired and intoxicated men. Marmeladov, now visibly more animated and disarrayed, continues his monologue to Raskolnikov. The mood is disrupted by a boisterous party entering: men and a girl with a concertina, all in threadbare festive dress. The new arrivals, already drunk, add noise and bustle. Other drinkers turn to watch and laugh. The overall mood is chaotic and bleak, darkened by smoke.
ch2-scene2: A late evening street in St. Petersburg, dim and cool, illuminated by the pale summer dusk. Raskolnikov, gaunt and serious, supports the staggering figure of Marmeladov as they trudge past shabby buildings. Marmeladov, wearing threadbare, mismatched clothes, leans on Raskolnikov, his face sorrowful and anxious. The buildings loom over them, their facades worn and covered in grime, with a dark entrance to Kozel’s house ahead.
ch2-scene3: A narrow, dilapidated upper-floor room lit by the flicker of a dying candle, with tattered children's clothes and rags strewn everywhere. Katerina Ivanovna, lean and feverish, paces the floor, her hands pressed to her chest; her dark hair is disheveled, her face flushed and gaunt. Three children—an eldest daughter comforting her brother, a small girl sleeping on the floor—huddle in the shadows. Marmeladov kneels by the threshold, while Raskolnikov, tense and withdrawn, stands just inside. The background reveals an open door with hostile and mocking faces peering in, and clouds of tobacco smoke drifting in from the louder adjoining rooms. The scene is tense and claustrophobic.
ch2-scene4: A shadowed stairwell in a dark, run-down St. Petersburg tenement. Raskolnikov, thin and still in his worn clothing, descends alone, his face tense with conflicted emotion. The lantern-light from below casts long, dramatic shadows on the grimy walls. Outside, the street is nearly empty as dusk falls, with the faint glow of the northern night sky. The atmosphere is somber and reflective, encapsulating his troubled thoughts as he walks away.
ch3-scene0: A cramped, drab garret room with peeling yellow wallpaper, low ceiling, rickety chairs, a cluttered table, and a battered old sofa serving as a bed. Dull morning light seeps in through a small, dirty window. Raskolnikov, gaunt and disheveled, sits up on the sofa wearing a threadbare student’s overcoat. Next to him, Nastasya, the talkative servant, stands holding a chipped teapot and sets a meager breakfast on the table. The atmosphere is grim and poverty-stricken, true to 19th-century St. Petersburg.
ch3-scene1: Inside the same small, dingy room, Raskolnikov sits alone on the worn sofa, letter in hand. His expression turns from anxious anticipation to distress as he raises the envelope to his lips before opening it. Faded daylight spills over scattered books and neglected belongings, emphasizing the isolation of the scene. The sense is heavy with emotion and expectation, typical of 1860s St. Petersburg impoverished lodgings.
ch3-scene2: Vignettes evoked by the letter: In a modest provincial home, Pulcheria Raskolnikov and Avdotya Romanovna (Dounia) are seen comforting one another. Elsewhere, in the garden of a prosperous rural estate, Svidrigaïlov accosts Dounia under Marfa Petrovna’s watchful, shocked gaze. Tension is palpable as Marfa strikes Dounia in the rain, with servants whispering at a distance. Clothing is formal yet provincial—modest dresses, Marfa in somber attire, Svidrigaïlov in a waistcoat and shirtsleeves. Lighting shifts from dour interiors to gray outdoor drizzle, evoking disgrace and reconciliation.
ch3-scene3: The cramped, dim garret at midday, Raskolnikov slumped, brow furrowed and tear-stained, alone on the squalid sofa. His fists clench the crumpled letter on his lap. Light streams weakly through the window, illuminating the dirty pillow beneath his head and casting long shadows that intensify the room’s oppressive, suffocating feel. The atmosphere is heavy, inward, and troubled.
ch3-scene4: Raskolnikov, haggard and distracted, walks briskly along the wide, bustling Vassilyevsky Prospect amid crowds in 1860s Petersburg. He mutters to himself, oblivious to the curious or wary glances of passing townsfolk—men in coats and caps, women in shawls, vendors, and shopkeepers. The summer sky is overcast, rendering the city’s facades pale and gloomy; energy and anxiety hang in the air.
ch4-scene0: An austere student room in 19th-century St. Petersburg, with faded wallpaper and sparse furnishings. Rodion Raskolnikov, gaunt and intense, dressed in shabby, threadbare clothes, paces or sits, lost in agonized thought, his weary face twisted in bitterness. The atmosphere is heavy and claustrophobic, lit only by pale daylight leaking through a small window.
ch4-scene1: A wide, sunlit boulevard nearly deserted in the stifling afternoon heat of St. Petersburg. Raskolnikov, unkempt and gaunt, moves toward a distant park bench under sparsely-leaved trees. A young, fair-haired girl in a disheveled, torn dress and no hat or gloves stumbles ahead of him. The surroundings exude a heavy, oppressive summer atmosphere.
ch4-scene2: On an isolated bench under leafy trees along the dusty boulevard, the drunken girl collapses in exhaustion, dress askew, while Raskolnikov pauses before her, his expression troubled. Across the boulevard, a plump, fashionably dressed dandy of about thirty watches the scene, poised to approach. The distant city architecture is half-shrouded in early afternoon haze.
ch4-scene3: In a moment of rising tension on the boulevard, Raskolnikov, thin and frantic, confronts the stout, well-dressed dandy who raises his cane in anger. Suddenly, a policeman in a blue uniform, mustached and stocky, intervenes, seizing Raskolnikov by the arm. The drunk girl remains slumped on the bench, oblivious. Sunlight slants through the trees, heightening the scene's intensity.
ch4-scene4: Raskolnikov, still in his ragged attire, animatedly gestures as he brings the uniformed policeman to the bench. The drunken girl, rumpled and disoriented, slouches in the corner of the bench. The dandy stands a little distance off, smoking and watching with ill-concealed impatience. Afternoon light filters through thin foliage, creating a fraught yet listless air.
ch4-scene5: The young drunken girl rises unsteadily from the park bench, moving away down the deserted boulevard with a dazed, determined step. The dandy shadows her from a distance along another path, while the policeman, resolute and upright, moves to follow and protect. Raskolnikov watches, left behind near the empty bench, all under the oppressive afternoon sky.
ch4-scene6: Raskolnikov sits in dejection alone on the empty boulevard bench, hands clasped and head bowed. The long corridor of the boulevard stretches empty, haunted by the heat and bathed in the dull glare of the sun. The sense of isolation and anguish pervades the air, with only Raskolnikov's ragged form punctuating the desolate scene.
ch4-scene7: On a sunlit, nearly deserted boulevard, Raskolnikov sits lost in thought on the aging bench, his posture slumped in weary self-contemplation. The scene is almost timeless; there are no other figures present, only the faint suggestion of St. Petersburg buildings in the hazy distance. The pervasive quiet is broken only by drifting dust motes caught in the afternoon light.
ch5-scene0: A gaunt young man, Rodion Raskolnikov, walks aimlessly through the dusty, winding streets of St. Petersburg beneath the pale afternoon sun. He is disheveled in dark, threadbare clothes, his expression tense and feverish. The city looms with tall, oppressive buildings, narrow alleys, and occasional passersby in 19th-century dress. The mood is restless and anxious, flecks of city grime under drifting clouds, with Raskolnikov pausing near stone fences and vacant lots.
ch5-scene1: Raskolnikov collapses among wild grasses beneath sparse birch trees on a remote spot on Petrovsky Island. Dappled late-afternoon sunlight filters through the leaves, casting patchy shadows over his huddled form. His clothes are wrinkled, his hair moist with sweat, and his face is pale. The landscape is lush and quiet, removed from the city’s bustle, with an atmosphere of stillness and exhaustion.
ch5-scene2: A small, flat provincial Russian town under a gray sky. A young boy, Raskolnikov at age seven, walks with his father along a dusty, winding rural road past a crowded tavern. Drunken townsfolk and peasants in colorful, threadbare holiday attire mill about, singing and laughing raucously. The wooden tavern stands beside open fields, with a distant copse on the horizon and a stone church with a green dome far in the background. The mood is both vivid and menacing, laden with the tense energy of a festival turning crude.
ch5-scene3: A clamorous gathering outside a ramshackle tavern, with men and women in peasant shirts, beaded headdresses, and colorful shawls crowded around a large wooden cart. In the harness stands an obviously frail sorrel mare, steam rising from her thin flanks. Mikolka, a bulky young peasant with a red face, stands in the cart wielding a whip, egged on by other peasants and two young men preparing whips. Amid laughter and jeers, an unnamed fat woman munches on nuts. The sunny, eastward-slanting light exaggerates both festivity and brutality in this charged moment.
ch5-scene4: The little sorrel mare is collapsing under a rain of savage blows. Peasants and young men in rustic garb surround her, some mid-swing with sticks, axes, or poles; Mikolka, wild-eyed, wields a crowbar. Raskolnikov as a sobbing child clings to the dying horse’s head through the blood, frantic and desperate, while his father and an old man in the crowd look on in horror and resignation. The crowd’s faces blur in a haze of violence and drunken frenzy, the dust and sunlight hanging thick over the tragic scene.
ch5-scene5: Morning light glimmers through the sparse canopy over Raskolnikov, now awake, huddled and trembling on the dew-damp grass beneath a slender birch. His face is pale, hair matted, and clothes askew, deeply shaken by his nightmare. The surroundings are hushed and fresh, the air heavy with an uneasy peace after delirium, while Raskolnikov clutches his head, alone and haunted.
ch5-scene6: Raskolnikov, gaunt and wan, leans weakly over the balustrade of a bridge spanning the Neva. The sun is low and blood-red in a glowing sky, its reflections gilding the river. The city skyline of St. Petersburg rises indistinctly beyond, bathed in luminescent dusk. He stands alone with tired yet luminous eyes, the breeze stirring his threadbare coat. The atmosphere is redemptive yet somber—a turning point suffused with a strange sense of relief.
ch5-scene7: The bustling Hay Market is winding down as dusk falls, booths and barrows being packed up by vendors in peasant working dress. At one corner, a humble huckster and his petite, lively wife pause in conversation with the tall, awkward Lizaveta Ivanovna, who stands clutching a bundle. Her clothes are plain and a little outgrown, her manner timid, eyes attentive. Raskolnikov passes nearby, thin and unnoticed, ears straining. Oil lamps flicker inside shop windows, the shifting colors of the twilight mingling with market debris, creating an air of secrecy and premonition.
ch5-scene8: Raskolnikov, drawn and pale, steps through the dim entrance of a decrepit St. Petersburg tenement as nighttime settles in. The hall is shrouded in shadow, with peeling wallpaper and a creaking staircase leading up into darkness. He walks in a trance-like state, overwhelmed, his posture sunken in defeat. The mood is heavy with inevitability and isolation, illuminated only by a weak candle or lantern glow from a distant door.
ch6-scene0: Inside a dim, smoky St. Petersburg tavern with rough wooden tables and worn chairs, evening light filters through the sooty windows. Raskolnikov, thin and pale, sits alone with a cup of tea, his posture withdrawn and tense. Nearby, at a neighboring table, a student in a threadbare coat and a young officer in a modest military uniform converse animatedly over their tea. The mood is tense and introspective, with muted colors and shadows emphasizing the 19th-century Russian atmosphere. Raskolnikov glances furtively at the pair, listening intently.
ch6-scene1: A shabby attic room lit only by the greyish twilight of an early St. Petersburg evening seeping through a small window. Raskolnikov, exhausted and feverish, collapses onto a narrow sofa without removing his clothes. Dusty shelves, chipped crockery, and a threadbare blanket evoke poverty. The atmosphere is heavy, silent, and oppressive with a palpable sense of isolation. The faint outlines of 19th-century furnishings barely illuminate the gloom.
ch6-scene2: Morning light filters weakly through the single small window into the cramped garret. Nastasya, dressed in a simple Russian servant's dress with an apron, stands beside Raskolnikov’s sofa holding a teapot and a coarse plate of bread. Raskolnikov looks pale and lethargic, barely roused from his sleep. The room appears cluttered yet bleak, with simple 19th-century peasant furnishings and a persistent sense of sickness and malaise.
ch6-scene3: The cramped, shadowed attic as the afternoon wanes. Raskolnikov sits hunched over in his worn overcoat, nervously sewing a cloth noose inside it. On a battered table lies a cleaned, folded shirt, a needle and thread, and a small, carefully wrapped parcel. His expression is strained and haunted. The setting is sparse, every corner showing neglect typical of impoverished student quarters in 19th-century St. Petersburg.
ch6-scene4: The communal kitchen with a stone floor and rows of laundry hanging overhead. Nastasya is present, stooping over a basket, dressed in working clothes, while Raskolnikov—furtive and tense—passes through, trying not to attract attention. The old, creaky doors and rough wooden counters enhance the feeling of shared, lower-class tenement life. The lighting is dim, coming from a single window, with the atmosphere thick with apprehension.
ch6-scene5: A dark, narrow entryway with the open, cluttered porter’s room visible just off the street. Raskolnikov, in his overcoat and hat, cautiously steps inside and stoops to retrieve an axe from beneath a bench. The sunlight from the yard barely penetrates this shadowy, utilitarian space cluttered with firewood and tools. The atmosphere is tense, silent, and on edge; the architecture and objects all distinctly Russian and period-appropriate.
ch6-scene6: Raskolnikov passes along a dingy street bordered by high tenement buildings, carriages and carts passing in the dusk. He moves carefully through the shadows cast by hulking wagons of hay, blending with the crowd, his coat tightly fastened. The city is alive but indifferent, bathed in the dusty golden light of evening. The sense of anticipation and anxiety is heightened by the narrow, labyrinthine alleys and the press of pedestrians in 19th-century attire.
ch6-scene7: Inside a gloomy tenement stairwell, Raskolnikov stands at the fourth-floor landing, his back to the peeling wallpaper and cracked woodwork. He presses his hand to his chest and pauses outside an old, battered door. The dimness is relieved only by a faint glimmer from a window on the stair landing. Opposite, the doors to other flats are closed, the corridor eerily silent and deserted. Raskolnikov’s features contort in anxiety as he reaches for the bell, the axe concealed under his coat.
ch7-scene0: A dimly-lit, narrow corridor in a St. Petersburg apartment house, the wooden floor worn, yellowish oil lamp light flickering from inside the battered, paint-chipped door. Raskolnikov, a gaunt, pale young man in a shabby dark coat and cap, stands tense and desperate, face part shadow, hand reaching toward the partly-opened door. Alyona Ivanovna, a small, wizened old woman in an old-fashioned, greasy dark dress and tattered shawl, peers suspiciously through the gap, clutching the door handle tightly. The atmosphere is oppressive and charged with dread.
ch7-scene1: A cramped, cluttered interior of a poor St. Petersburg flat, lit by a small oil lamp on the table casting hard shadows. Faded icons and religious images on the wall, an old chest of drawers, a heavy table with buttons and bric-a-brac. Alyona Ivanovna confronts Raskolnikov, her face a mixture of fear and suspicion, standing near the doorway. Raskolnikov stands awkwardly by the table, holding a small wrapped parcel, his face pale and sweating. The air is thick, stifling, and tense.
ch7-scene2: Beside a closed window covered by grimy curtains, Alyona Ivanovna stands hunched over, fumbling with a knotted parcel by the scant daylight filtering in. Raskolnikov, half in shadow, stands a few paces away, concealed axe under his coat, trembling and watching her. The furniture is sparse, with a patched bed cover visible through an open bedroom doorway. The atmosphere is taut with impending violence.
ch7-scene3: The moment of brutal violence in a stifling, dimly lit main room. Raskolnikov, face twisted with desperation, swings a heavy axe down toward Alyona Ivanovna, who recoils, eyes wide with terror, mouth open in a silent cry. The room’s simple furnishings, religious images, and clutter cast harsh shadows. Alyona’s thin, greasy hair is disheveled, her small body almost merging with the battered floor. The lighting is lurid, emphasizing blood splattering near a bundle of old keys and a purse.
ch7-scene4: Raskolnikov kneels over Alyona Ivanovna’s lifeless body, rifling through her pockets in a feverish haste, careful to avoid the spreading pool of blood. The carpet and floor are stained deep red, keys glinting in his hand. The main room is cramped, walls lined with icons. He darts into the dimly lit bedroom, bumping the ornate chest and a low bed with a bright silk patchwork quilt. The entire scene is shadowy, tense, and grim.
ch7-scene5: In a cramped bedroom lined with Orthodox icons, Raskolnikov, pale and sweating, fumbles with a ring of keys before a red-leather-covered trunk under the bed. Blood stains are visible on his hands and coat. Silk dresses and clothes are tossed aside, gold trinkets and small cases scattered about. Weak daylight filters through thick curtains, heightening the sense of claustrophobia and anxiety.
ch7-scene6: Lizaveta Ivanovna, a tall, simple-minded young woman in a shabby long dress and kerchief, stands frozen at the centre of the room, bundle in her arms, wide-eyed in terror at her sister’s corpse on the floor. Raskolnikov, wild-eyed and dishevelled, bursts from the bedroom wielding the axe. The lamp casts grotesque, flickering shadows, and the entire scene is steeped in horror.
ch7-scene7: In the shadowy, cramped kitchen of the flat, Raskolnikov frantically washes blood from his hands and an axe in a battered metal pail atop a wooden bench, soap and rag beside him. The air is thick with dread and the ghostly gleam of lamp light. In the small adjacent entryway, discarded belongings and blood stains heighten the sense of disorder and panic.
ch7-scene8: A worn wooden staircase winding up the narrow, enclosed stairwell of an old St. Petersburg tenement. Raskolnikov, tense and pale, stands at the door of Alyona’s flat, peering down the dimly lit stairs, his form half-concealed by shadows. Faint voices and heavy footsteps echo from below, the cracked walls and faded railings bathed in the sickly gleam of a single sconce.
ch7-scene9: On the cramped, fourth-floor landing, just outside Alyona Ivanovna’s battered flat door, stout Koch, a middle-aged man in an old waistcoat and cap, pulls fiercely at the door handle, his face flushed and sweating. Beside him, a young man in a neat but threadbare student’s frock coat stands alert, eyes wide, gesturing to Koch, his posture intent. Inside, Raskolnikov is hidden, clutching the axe behind the closed door. Lamplight cast from the stair window creates sharp contrasts, stoking the tension.
ch7-scene10: On the gloomy, aging landing, Koch remains uneasily at the door, peering through the keyhole in vain, his large frame hunched and impatient. Faint light from the landing window strikes his nervous face as he fiddles with the handle and listens intently for any sign from within the stout, paint-chipped door. The air feels heavy with suspicion and impending discovery.
ch7-scene11: A winding, poorly lit staircase in a decrepit St. Petersburg apartment house. Raskolnikov, face ghostly pale, slips hurriedly and furtively down the stairs. Below, loud voices and running footsteps indicate chaos. The empty painters’ flat on the second floor—its door wide open—shows paint buckets and brushes scattered amid bare walls. The atmosphere is frantic, surging with dread and confusion.
ch7-scene12: A shadowy street near the canal in St. Petersburg, cobbles slick with recent rain, lamplight flickering off the water. Raskolnikov moves swiftly through the crowd, sweating and exhausted, pulling his battered coat tightly around him. Passersby in shabby peasant and working-class dress pay him little heed; distant outlines of carriages and crumbling tenement facades form the backdrop. The night is dense, muggy with a sense of pursuit.
ch7-scene13: A dim entry hall with the rough wooden door of the porter’s lodge ajar, faint lamplight spilling onto the stone floor. Raskolnikov, ghostlike in dusty overcoat and boots, quickly hides the axe under a bench, expression vacant with shock. The corridor is empty, landlady’s door closed. He stumbles up the dark stair to his cramped student garret, collapsing on the sagging sofa amid peeling walls and meagre furnishings. The room is oppressive, haunted by fear and exhaustion.
ch8-scene0: A narrow, dimly lit garret with peeling wallpaper and sparse, threadbare furnishings. Early morning light creeps through a dirty window, illuminating Rodion Raskolnikov lying disheveled on a worn sofa, his coat draped over him, various items scattered in disarray around him. The scene is fraught with tension and feverish energy, set in St. Petersburg circa 1860s. The air is heavy, conveying Raskolnikov's restless turmoil and anxiety.
ch8-scene1: Inside Raskolnikov's cramped room, brightened by late morning sunlight, Raskolnikov sits up anxiously on his sofa, clutching rags in his hand. At the doorway, Nastasya, a robust, middle-aged woman in a plain dress and apron, stands with her fist raised from knocking, her face stern but concerned. The porter, a sturdy man in workman's attire with a peaked cap, stands beside her, holding out a folded paper. The room remains sparse and untidy, suffused with an urgent, unsettled mood.
ch8-scene2: A dim and narrow staircase leads down from the cheap tenement, the wood worn from traffic. Raskolnikov, pale and unsteady, descends the steps, one hand on the railing, the other clutching his hat. Outside, a bustling city street bathes in harsh, summery light reflecting off the dust. People in 19th-century Russian attire move past drays, cabs, and market stalls; the air vibrates with city life yet feels oppressive for the feverish young man making his way through the crowd.
ch8-scene3: Inside the police station of a newly built St. Petersburg apartment block, a dreary, low-ceilinged hallway is filled with waiting peasants, porters with books under their arms, and tired clerks in modest coats. Raskolnikov, gaunt and anxious, stands uncertainly among the crowd. The walls are freshly painted, the air stuffy with heat and the pungent scent of oil and perspiration. Period police uniforms and worn peasant dress offer visual contrast in this cramped, bureaucratic setting.
ch8-scene4: A small, cluttered government office crowded with people. The head clerk, a young man with fashionable clothes and neatly parted hair, sits at a table with documents. Two ladies are present: one in mourning dress writes at the table; the other, Luise Ivanovna, extravagantly dressed in a blue and white lace gown, stands nervously by, her dress almost filling the cramped space. Raskolnikov, rumpled and ill, hands a paper to the head clerk. The room is stifling and tense, with period uniforms and somber, early afternoon light.
ch8-scene5: In the cramped police office, the flamboyant assistant superintendent enters with exaggerated bravado, tossing his cockaded cap on the table and settling into an easy chair. His reddish moustache and officer’s uniform contrast with the somber mood. Luise Ivanovna, flustered in her voluminous gown, curtsies beside him. Raskolnikov stands nearby, looking pale and affronted. Other clerks and petitioners linger in the background amidst scattered papers. The tension, embarrassment, and hierarchy of the 19th-century bureaucracy are palpable.
ch8-scene6: The assistant superintendent, visibly furious, berates Luise Ivanovna in front of the assembled clerks and petitioners. She tries to defend herself, swathed in her extravagant dress, in contrast to the rigid uniforms of the police. The atmosphere is increasingly heated and chaotic, highlighted by harsh light from an upper window. Raskolnikov stands to the side, nervously amused, while the head clerk watches with anxious resignation. The period details—brass buttons, feathered hats, inkwells—lend historical authenticity to the turmoil.
ch8-scene7: As Luise Ivanovna exits, she bumps into Nikodim Fomitch, the superintendent: a well-built, affable man with impressive fair whiskers and a smart uniform, just entering the office. Luise Ivanovna, flustered, curtsies deeply before hurriedly leaving. Ilya Petrovitch (assistant superintendent) stands nearby, still bristling. Raskolnikov and the head clerk look on. The setting is cluttered with paperwork and official portraits under the unforgiving glow of midday light, capturing the bustle of officialdom.
ch8-scene8: Raskolnikov, pale and feverish, stands before Nikodim Fomitch and the assistant superintendent, explaining his financial and personal circumstances. The head clerk listens with a skeptical smile, while the officers, one affable and one prickly, exhibit a mixture of interest and impatience. Other petitioners and clerks work or wait in the periphery. The air is heavy, the atmosphere tense with bureaucracy and exhaustion. Period uniforms and office clutter complete the scene.
ch8-scene9: Seated at the table, Raskolnikov grips a pen unsteadily, struggling to write as sweat beads on his brow. The head clerk, matter-of-fact and indifferent, dictates the standard declaration, while the other officers go about their business or glance over with detached curiosity. Papers, ink, and official stamps crowd the desk. The oppressive heat and bureaucratic monotony weigh heavily, with early afternoon sunlight struggling through dusty windows.
ch8-scene10: Nikodim Fomitch and Ilya Petrovitch stand together in animated discussion near the clerk’s desk, speaking about the circumstances of a recent murder investigation. Nearby, Raskolnikov, pale and visibly ill, listens intently but suddenly appears overwhelmed and on the verge of collapse. Other clerks and seekers in period dress populate the background, the oppressive heat and tension heightening the stifling, anxious atmosphere.
ch8-scene11: With Raskolnikov seated and revived, still pale, Nikodim Fomitch stands in front of him with an expression of concern, while another clerk supports him on one side and an attendant offers a glass of water on the other. The assistant superintendent, now at his desk, glances over with measured suspicion as the head clerk resumes his work. The office’s stifling air and subdued light underscore the discomfort and formality of the moment.
ch8-scene12: On the bustling St. Petersburg street outside the police station, Raskolnikov emerges, pale but somewhat steadier, into the afternoon sunlight. The street is alive with 19th-century pedestrians—workmen, clerks, and city dwellers going about their business. The new stone façade of the police building towers behind him, symbolizing the omnipresent authority. The urban background is dusty and noisy, reinforcing Raskolnikov's sense of anxiety and growing panic as he heads home.
ch9-scene0: A dingy, small garret room with faded wallpaper, sparse old furniture, and a narrow bed. The midday light filters dimly through a small window, casting pale rectangles across the worn floorboards. Raskolnikov, gaunt and disheveled in rumpled 19th-century Russian attire, hastily stuffs small objects into the pockets of his overcoat, his expression anxious and frantic. The mood is tense, the setting cramped, and no one else is present.
ch9-scene1: A dusty embankment along the canal lined with weathered stone steps leading down to the water, bustling with laundresses washing clothes and various townspeople. Barges and small boats cluster at the water’s edge. Raskolnikov in his threadbare coat and cap paces tensely, glancing around warily amidst the ordinary St. Petersburg workers in simple peasant and urban garb. The light is flat and overcast, the atmosphere anxious amid everyday bustle.
ch9-scene2: A grimy, secluded yard surrounded by high, blank stone walls and a wooden hoarding, the ground dirty and littered with rubbish. At one end, a soot-blackened stone shed, likely part of a workshop, can be glimpsed. Against the wall, a large unhewn stone sits beside a crude wooden sink with a comic warning chalked above. Raskolnikov, alone and tense, bends down to lift the stone and hurriedly deposits small items into the hollow earth. The industrial atmosphere is shabby and shadowed, the only figure present is Raskolnikov.
ch9-scene3: An open cobblestone square bordered by early evening shadows. Raskolnikov, agitated and thin, traverses the expanse amidst the faint presence of walking townsfolk, their forms blurry and indistinct. The setting transitions from open square to the tree-lined boulevard, the street lamps not yet lit, dusk approaching. His posture is tense, his expression haunted. Passing figures (blurry) emphasize his isolation, 1860s St. Petersburg attire visible.
ch9-scene4: At the stone bridge over the bright blue Neva beneath a late afternoon sky, Raskolnikov stands near the balustrade, pausing and looking across the water towards ornate rooftops and the shining cupola of the distant cathedral. The quiet stretch of the embankment contrasts with the lively city in the background. Raskolnikov, haggard and thin in his dark coat, contemplates the water, appearing isolated against the vast cityscape.
ch9-scene5: A cramped and shabby garret room with sloped ceiling, tattered furniture, a battered desk strewn with papers, and an old sofa. Razumihin, unshaven and large in a threadbare dressing gown and slippers, stands by the open door, surprised. Raskolnikov, visibly ill and exhausted, sits heavily on the broken sofa, while Razumihin gestures animatedly nearby. The wan afternoon light filters in from a small window, heightening the squalid, intimate feel.
ch9-scene6: A narrow wooden stairwell lit by faint daylight seeping in through grimy windows, the steps worn and railings chipped. Raskolnikov, silent and pale, descends with uncertain steps; behind him Razumihin appears at the landing, shouting after him. The scene transitions to a bustling street outside, with Raskolnikov pushing past passersby in their varied coats, the stone facades of St. Petersburg looming in evening light.
ch9-scene7: The broad Nikolaevsky Bridge arches over the Neva amid the evening bustle. Horse-drawn carriages move through the traffic, a coachman in livery whipping his horses as Raskolnikov, dazed, stumbles dangerously in their path. Passersby in various 1860s urban garments – a mix of workers, ladies with parasols, and merchants – look on, some laughing. The scene is colored by the fading light, and among the crowd are an elderly woman in a kerchief and a young girl with a green parasol, pausing beside Raskolnikov.
ch9-scene8: Raskolnikov stands alone at the center of the bridge, gripping a small coin in his fist, facing the broad river and ornate domes of St. Petersburg's skyline. The declining sun glints off the water and the gilded dome of the distant cathedral, details crisp in the clear air. Raskolnikov's figure is solitary and thin, lost in thought as he prepares to cast the coin into the river, surrounded by the serenity and grandeur of 19th-century St. Petersburg.
ch9-scene9: The small, darkening room is cloaked in shadow as dusk deepens outside the single window. Raskolnikov, exhausted and pale, lies on his narrow sofa, his overcoat over him, in disarray after a long day. The atmosphere is one of fatigue and gloom, the objects in the room barely discernible in the fading light, deepening the air of isolation.
ch9-scene10: Gloomy dusk inside Raskolnikov’s cluttered room as he jolts upright on his sofa, horror on his face. Echoing faintly, the dimly lit communal stairwell outside is crowded: the landlady, unseen but her screams suggested, and the indistinct forms of various tenants rushing out. The imagined figure of Ilya Petrovitch (not an available entity) is only implied by chaos and noise. The mood is feverish, chaotic, and filled with confusion.
ch9-scene11: The tiny room, now shadowed in dusk, is illuminated by a single flickering candle brought in by Nastasya, a sturdy working woman in a simple headscarf and apron. She stands beside the rickety table, placing a plate of soup and bread. Raskolnikov lies weakly under his coat on the bed, his face pale and drawn, looking at her with confusion. The modest furnishings and sparse decor underscore the poverty and care in the moment.
ch10-scene0: A cramped, dimly lit St. Petersburg garret, the sunlight filtering in to illuminate a bed where a feverish young man lies in disarray. Around him linger vague, shadowy figures – Nastasya with simple servant attire and another indistinct presence – watching nervously. The atmosphere is stifling, anxious, and blurred by the haze of delirium, evoking a sense of illness and confusion typical of a 19th-century Russian student’s room in poverty.
ch10-scene1: The same threadbare garret now sharp in the golden slant of the morning sun. Raskolnikov sits up on the bed, pale and bewildered. Beside him stands Nastasya, dressed in a faded dress and apron, and a young bearded man dressed as an office messenger in modest, short-waisted coat. The stout, shy landlady peeks hesitantly through the half-open door. The small, cluttered room radiates humble realism and concerned anticipation.
ch10-scene2: In the morning-lit garret, Razumihin—a tall, vigorous student clad in slightly shabby but neat clothes—bursts in energetically, immediately filling the space. Raskolnikov sits on the bed, flanked by Nastasya and the bearded messenger. The landlady lingers by the door, her expression wary. The room, lined with sparse 19th-century furnishings, pulses with the bustle of recent wakefulness and the camaraderie, tension, and awkwardness of a gathering around the sick man.
ch10-scene3: Daylight fills the small garret as Nastasya returns with soup and sets a sparse but clean table. Razumihin sits close to Raskolnikov, supporting him on the sofa and feeding him soup with gentle insistence. The cluttered table bears plates, bottles of beer, and tea implements. Nastasya stands nearby, hands occupied. The room's details—plain wallpaper, patched furnishings—reflect humble circumstances. The scene is intimate, quietly tender, yet heavy with the patient’s apprehension and the nurse-like attention of his concerned friends.
ch10-scene4: The garret is crowded but buoyant as Razumihin, energetic and expressive, sits at the table recounting stories. He gestures animatedly, while Nastasya balances a saucer, grinning with amusement; Raskolnikov listens warily, partially reclined on the bed, visibly tense among the warmth and vibrancy. The meal remnants linger, and the room feels alive with conversation, laughter, and the life of St. Petersburg’s poorer intellectual class.
ch10-scene5: After Razumihin leaves, Nastasya stands at the door, curiosity tugging her as she listens then hurries down the stairs. Raskolnikov, abruptly alone, leaps from his bed in agitation. The small garret appears chaotic, with the quilt tossed aside and scattered clues—frayed clothes, an empty beer bottle—evident in the morning light. The sense of paranoia, loneliness, and impending action is palpable in the air.
ch10-scene6: Evening descends in the garret; dusky blue shadows stripe the room. Raskolnikov, just awake, sits dazed on the bed as Razumihin stands in the threshold, arms full of a loosely wrapped parcel. Nastasya, summoned, brings in the bundle. The sparse furniture and familiar disorder frame the act of gentle but meddling friendship, with period-appropriate working and student class attire lending authenticity to the poignant domestic moment.
ch11-scene0: In a cramped, dimly lit St. Petersburg student lodging, three men gather around a shabby sofa. Raskolnikov, gaunt and pale, lies reclined, facing the wall, covered in a worn blanket; Razumihin, robust and energetic, sits beside him, leaning forward anxiously. The newly arrived Zossimov, formally dressed in a light grey summer coat and spectacles, positions himself with composed authority at the foot of the sofa. The room is littered, with yellowed wallpaper and patches of sunlight filtering through a narrow window. Nastasya stands unobtrusively by the doorway, observing the scene, her simple maid’s attire contrasting with the men’s period suits. The air is thick with tension and concern.
ch12-scene0: A cramped, dimly lit, shabby Petersburg student room with peeling wallpaper and a grimy window. Raskolnikov, disheveled and pale, lies on a threadbare sofa, dressed in a loose shirt and unkempt. Razumihin sits upright nearby in modest attire, facing the door, while Zossimov, in a doctor's frock coat, appears lethargic and bored. Pyotr Petrovitch Luzhin stands uncertainly in the doorway, dressed elegantly in a new fawn summer jacket, stylish hat in hand, impeccable cravat, and lavender gloves, surveying the untidy room with haughty distaste. The scene is tense; sunlight filters weakly through the grimy panes.
ch12-scene1: The setting remains Raskolnikov’s dilapidated room. Luzhin now threads his way in and sits awkwardly on a mismatched chair near the sofa, holding his delicate gloves and hat. Razumihin, sturdy and boisterous, creates space at the small wooden table, his posture a blend of courtesy and territoriality. Zossimov, pale and bored, lounges further back, partly disengaged. Raskolnikov sits up on the sofa, pale with forced defiance in his unshaven face, focusing an agitated gaze upon Luzhin. The light dims as clouds cover the window, compounding the thick, uneasy atmosphere.
ch12-scene2: Luzhin, upright in his chair, expounds with visible self-importance, cradling his hat and gloves, eyes scanning the room for impact. Razumihin challenges him animatedly, gesticulating and leaning forward, his open shirt sleeves rolled up. Zossimov, dryly disengaged, leans back languidly. Raskolnikov lies back on his sofa, hands behind his head, fixed on the ceiling in sullen agitation. The light remains diffuse and somber, underscoring the intellectual tension and class contrast among the characters, all in period-appropriate Russian dress.
ch12-scene3: Tension fills the cluttered student quarters. Luzhin rises, hat and gloves clutched, his face flushed with offense and indignation. He stands near the exit, preparing to leave and avoiding glances at the others. Raskolnikov lies rigid on the bed, ashen and sharp-eyed, lips trembling. Razumihin half-stands, blocking the chair to ease Luzhin’s exit, unsettled. Zossimov, indifferent but watchful, makes a slight gesture toward the door. The room is cast in sullen, late afternoon gloom.
ch12-scene4: Dimly illuminated by stray sunlight filtering through a filthy stairwell window, Zossimov descends the narrow, well-worn wooden stairs, gesturing for Razumihin to follow. Razumihin pauses on the landing, slightly stooped, concerned and animated in simple student clothes. The stairway is claustrophobic, with peeling walls and rough banisters typical of St. Petersburg tenements, carrying the somber mood of unresolved tension.
ch12-scene5: The tiny, shadowy room feels emptier. Raskolnikov, pale and agitated, turns away on his sofa, his back to the viewer, shakily resisting attention. Nastasya stands nearby, dressed in her plain servant’s garb with a kerchief over her hair, hands folded, hesitating before quietly withdrawing. The furniture is sparse and battered. A feeling of isolation and exhaustion pervades the air, enhanced by the fading evening light.
ch13-scene0: A dim, cramped St. Petersburg garret room with a small bed, a wooden table with coins and a package atop it, and threadbare furniture. The setting sun casts reddish light through a dirty window. Raskolnikov, gaunt and pale in newly bought but modest 19th-century clothes, moves silently by the door, glancing toward the kitchen. In the corridor, a glimpse of Nastasya stands at a stove tending the samovar, her back turned.
ch13-scene1: The narrow, dusty street glows with the orange hue of late sunset. Raskolnikov steps outside amidst the bustle of St. Petersburg, surrounded by shabby tenements, with smoke and dust thick in the air. The atmosphere is oppressive and stifling, with a faint, feverish energy emanating from the protagonist as he begins his purposeful stride into the evening.
ch13-scene2: On a crowded St. Petersburg street near the market, a dark-haired street musician with a barrel organ stands by a shop, while a poorly dressed young girl in an old crinoline, faded mantle, and feathered straw hat sings. Passersby linger, and Raskolnikov, thin and shadowy, stands among them, dropping a coin into the girl's hand. The scene is lively, colored with urban poverty.
ch13-scene3: Amidst the busy, open market square, Raskolnikov stops beside a corn chandler’s stall to question a young man in a red shirt. Market booths and passersby surround them; the air is filled with noisy commerce as dusk settles over the square.
ch13-scene4: A thick crowd of peasants in simple provincial dress throngs a corner of the dusty market square. Raskolnikov stands amid the jostling bodies, searching faces with nervous intent. Chatter and shouting fill the air, and the scents and clamor of the lower class marketplace pervade the dusk.
ch13-scene5: The street outside a noisy, lower-floor tavern, beneath tenement blocks. Bareheaded women in cotton dresses and goatskin shoes cluster about the tavern entrance, some sitting on steps, others standing in groups with bruised faces. Raskolnikov lingers at the threshold, peering in at the candlelit interior where rough singing and guitar music echo. A drunken soldier wanders near, and a beggar quarrels with another nearby. The chaotic scene is grimy and boisterous.
ch13-scene6: A clean, well-lit 19th-century restaurant with polished floors and large windows. Scattered guests sit at white-covered tables; in one room, four men enjoy champagne. At another table, Raskolnikov, haggard in his new suit, pores over a stack of newspapers, looking tense. Waiters in white aprons serve tea. Zametov, in a stylish but slightly worn waistcoat, approaches and sits with Raskolnikov, his face cheerful but flushed from drink.
ch13-scene7: On the wide stone steps of the Palais de Cristal, the twilight deepens as Raskolnikov, pale and agitated, nearly collides with Razumihin, who stands broad-shouldered and angry. Both are clad in modest 19th-century suits, illuminated by a faint lamplight from the entrance. The city street stretches behind them into the dusk.
ch13-scene8: The broad stone bridge arches over a canal, reflecting the deepening pink-and-purple hues of sunset. Raskolnikov leans heavily on the parapet, his face shadowed and weary. Close by, an elderly woman in a kerchief stands with a haunted expression, her features ravaged by despair. Passers-by gather in the distance, and the quiet waters glimmer below.
ch13-scene9: On the granite steps of the canal embankment, a commotion surrounds the limp, drenched form of the elderly woman, recently rescued from the water by a constable in shirtsleeves. She sits coughing and sneezing, while a distraught neighbor kneels beside her, and a cluster of onlookers, some weeping, press around. Twilight darkens the scene, lit sporadically by lanterns and the remaining glow of sunset.
ch13-scene10: Raskolnikov wanders in the gloom along narrow cobblestone streets, lined with fading stucco buildings and gas lamps flickering to life. The atmosphere is somber, almost airless, with deepening shadow and few passersby. He pauses, staring dazedly at the entrance of a dark, familiar tenement—Alyona Ivanovna's house—drawn by an existential compulsion.
ch13-scene11: A cramped, fourth-floor St. Petersburg apartment in transition: furniture gone, two young workmen in shirtsleeves hurriedly roll up wallpaper, with new white-lilac patterns replacing old yellowed walls. Raskolnikov sits listlessly on the window-sill, gazing around with a haunted look. The last rays of twilight barely penetrate the small, grimy windows.
ch13-scene12: Dim light flickers over a grimy communal entrance of a large tenement. The two porters, burly in ill-fitting coats with keys at their belts, stand with crossed arms; a peasant woman in a kerchief scolds nearby, and a mysterious man in a long coat watches from the shadows. The workmen, just exited, linger nearby. Raskolnikov stands among them, looking remote and detached. Nightfall creeps over the cobbled street beyond.
ch13-scene13: Under the dim, blue glow of early night, Raskolnikov pauses alone at the center of a cobblestone crossroads, encircled by empty streets and lifeless buildings. A distant crowd, gathered around a carriage and lantern, is just visible down one street. The atmosphere is eerie and heavy with isolation.
ch14-scene0: A bustling nighttime city street in 19th-century St. Petersburg under flickering gas lanterns. Crowds of anxious onlookers, including the police and a coachman wringing his hands, gather around the wheel of a fine carriage. Policemen with lanterns illuminate a bloodied, unconscious man on the cobblestones. Faces are tense and pale; people jostle for a look, attired in worn coats and shawls, with an air of shock and morbid curiosity.
ch14-scene1: A dim, shabby room with peeling wallpaper and sparse furniture in a St. Petersburg tenement, lit by a faint single candle and some lantern light from the hall. Katerina Ivanovna, gaunt and flushed, paces the room wrapped in a thin shawl as Polenka undresses a sickly little boy on a chair, while a younger girl waits in rags by a folding screen. The chaotic atmosphere is heavy with exhaustion and poverty.
ch14-scene2: The previously quiet, cramped room suddenly fills with people as men carry in the bloodied, unconscious Marmeladov and lay him on a threadbare sofa. Katerina Ivanovna gasps in white-faced horror while her children huddle in fear. Raskolnikov, breathless and urgent, directs helpers, as a policeman stands to the side. The air is tense and frantic, lit harshly by lanterns and candlelight.
ch14-scene3: A packed, stifling tenement room as curious neighbors crowd the doorway and press inside, smoke drifting in from the hall. Katerina Ivanovna angrily scolds the gawkers and stands protectively by Marmeladov. Madame Lippevechsel, a stern, formidable landlady, confronts her in the doorway; Mr. Lebeziatnikov peeks in, amused. The desperate, chaotic energy contrasts with the dim, uneven candlelight and flickers of lanterns from the corridor.
ch14-scene4: The dim room grows tense as a precise, elderly German doctor in dark frock coat arrives and examines Marmeladov, aided by Katerina Ivanovna. The policeman stands by quietly as the candle flickers over the bloody, dying man. The atmosphere is thick with fear and finality; weary faces of the family, Raskolnikov, and the silent children are bathed in the uneven glow.
ch14-scene5: A sorrowful, candle-lit room as the frail priest in vestments gives last rites to dying Marmeladov surrounded by his family on their knees in prayer. Katerina Ivanovna, hunched and exhausted, holds her children close. Sonia, pale and trembling in gaudy, tattered finery and feathered hat, stands hesitantly in the doorway. Faces in the crowd are illuminated by the single candle and the glimmer from the hall. The mood is one of solemnity, grief, and desperate prayer.
ch14-scene6: A shadowy stairwell with gaslight pooling in the corners as Raskolnikov pushes out from the press of mourners at Marmeladov's door. Nikodim Fomitch, in uniform, encounters him in the stairwell as a priest descends behind. The fatigue and grief are etched in Raskolnikov's pale, blood-spattered face. The atmosphere is heavy with weary relief.
ch14-scene7: The dim base of a cramped stone stairwell, lit by a faint street lantern through a grungy window. Raskolnikov, pale and unsteady, pauses as Polenka—thin, barefoot, and earnest—runs up to him smiling, passing on Sonia’s message. The atmosphere is tender and intimate, tinged with childish sincerity and gratitude.
ch14-scene8: A moonlit, deserted St. Petersburg bridge arches over inky water under a lamplit sky. Raskolnikov stands motionless, staring down contemplatively at the river, the city night stretching behind him. His silhouette is gaunt and solitary, framed by the empty expanse and chill air, embodying newfound resolution.
ch14-scene9: At a noisy landing outside a large, brightly-lit apartment, Raskolnikov stands among serving maids bustling past with samovars and trays. The door to Razumihin’s lively room is wide open, revealing many guests inside. Razumihin, flushed and cheerful, emerges to greet Raskolnikov, who stands pale, disheveled, and weary amidst the hubbub.
ch14-scene10: A quiet nighttime street glistening with recent rain, illuminated by sparse gaslights. Raskolnikov staggers, supported by a talkative Razumihin; both are wrapped in dark coats. Their faces are intent, Razumihin animated with drunken cheer, Raskolnikov pale and exhausted. The empty, silent city stretches behind them.
ch14-scene11: A shadow-filled stairwell inside a decaying Petersburg boarding house. At the foot of the narrow stairs, Razumihin and Raskolnikov pause, both gazing upward at a line of warm light glowing beneath the garret door above. The setting is drab and claustrophobic, with anticipation in the air.
ch14-scene12: A small, bleak attic room under the eaves, lit by a solitary oil lamp. Pulcheria Alexandrovna and Dounia, elegantly dressed but weary, sit anxiously on the meager sofa, Nastasya stands by, while Raskolnikov and Razumihin enter. The women leap up and embrace Raskolnikov, who collapses, pale and overcome, in their arms. The atmosphere is charged with relief and shock, tears glistening in the gentle lamplight.
